summary: As a key form in online social platforms, group chat is a popular space for interest exchange or problem-solving, but its effectiveness is often hindered by inactivity and management challenges. While recent large language models (LLMs) have powered impressive one-to-one conversational agents, their seamlessly integration into multi-participant conversations remains unexplored. To address this gap, we introduce GCAgent, an LLM-driven system for enhancing group chats communication with both entertainment- and utility-oriented dialogue agents. The system comprises three tightly integrated modules: Agent Builder, which customizes agents to align with users' interests; Dialogue Manager, which coordinates dialogue states and manage agent invocations; and Interface Plugins, which reduce interaction barriers by three distinct tools. Through extensive experiment, GCAgent achieved an average score of 4.68 across various criteria and was preferred in 51.04\% of cases compared to its base model. Additionally, in real-world deployments over 350 days, it increased message volume by 28.80\%, significantly improving group activity and engagement. Overall, this work presents a practical blueprint for extending LLM-based dialogue agent from one-party chats to multi-party group scenarios.

AI.tldr: GCAgent is an LLM-driven system that introduces entertainment- and utility-oriented dialogue agents to enhance group chat communication by addressing inactivity and management challenges in multi-participant conversations.
AI.motivation: Group chats in online platforms are popular for interest exchange and problem-solving but suffer from inactivity and management challenges. While LLMs have powered effective one-to-one conversational agents, their integration into multi-participant conversations remains unexplored.
AI.method: GCAgent consists of three integrated modules: Agent Builder for customizing agents aligned with users' interests, Dialogue Manager for coordinating dialogue states and managing agent invocations, and Interface Plugins with three distinct tools to reduce interaction barriers.
AI.result: GCAgent achieved an average score of 4.68 across various criteria, was preferred in 51.04% of cases compared to its base model, and in real-world deployments over 350 days, increased message volume by 28.80%, significantly improving group activity and engagement.
AI.conclusion: This work presents a practical blueprint for extending LLM-based dialogue agents from one-party chats to multi-party group scenarios, demonstrating effectiveness in enhancing group chat communication.

title: WaterSIC: information-theoretically (near) optimal linear layer quantization
summary: This paper considers the problem of converting a given dense linear layer to low precision. The tradeoff between compressed length and output discrepancy is analyzed information theoretically (IT). It is shown that a popular GPTQ algorithm may have an arbitrarily large gap to the IT limit. To alleviate this problem, a novel algorithm, termed ''WaterSIC'', is proposed and is shown to be within a rate gap of 0.255 bits to the IT limit, uniformly over all possible covariance matrices of input activations. The key innovation of WaterSIC's is to allocate different quantization rates to different columns (in-features) of the weight matrix, mimicking the classical IT solution known as ''waterfilling''. Applying WaterSIC to the Llama and Qwen family of LLMs establishes new state-of-the-art performance for all quantization rates from 1 to 4 bits.

AI.tldr: WaterSIC algorithm improves neural network weight quantization by allocating different precision levels to different weight columns, achieving near-optimal compression while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the problem of compressing dense linear layers in neural networks to low precision (quantization). Current methods like GPTQ can have arbitrarily large gaps from the information-theoretic optimal limit, motivating the need for better quantization algorithms.
AI.method: Proposes a novel algorithm called WaterSIC that mimics classical information-theory "waterfilling" solution. It allocates different quantization rates to different columns (in-features) of the weight matrix rather than using uniform quantization across all weights.
AI.result: WaterSIC achieves a rate gap of only 0.255 bits to the information-theoretic limit, uniformly over all possible covariance matrices of input activations. When applied to Llama and Qwen family LLMs, it establishes new state-of-the-art performance for all quantization rates from 1 to 4 bits.
AI.conclusion: WaterSIC provides a theoretically sound and practically effective approach to neural network weight quantization that significantly outperforms existing methods like GPTQ, demonstrating the value of applying information-theoretic principles to neural network compression.

title: X-RAY: Mapping LLM Reasoning Capability via Formalized and Calibrated Probes
summary: Large language models (LLMs) achieve promising performance, yet their ability to reason remains poorly understood. Existing evaluations largely emphasize task-level accuracy, often conflating pattern matching with reasoning capability. We present X-RAY, an explainable reasoning analysis system that maps the LLM reasoning capability using calibrated, formally verified probes. We model reasoning capability as a function of extractable \textit{structure}, operationalized through formal properties such as constraint interaction, reasoning depth, and solution-space geometry. X-Ray generates probes via formal tools with controlled structural variations, enabling precise isolation of incremental structural information through formal calibration and verification. We evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs on problems ranging from junior-level to advanced in mathematics, physics, and chemistry. Our analysis reveals a systematic asymmetry in LLM reasoning: models are relatively robust to constraint refinement, where additional conditions shrink an existing solution space, but degrade sharply under solution-space restructuring, where modifications alter the underlying structural form of the solution manifold. Moreover, calibrated formal probes differentiate models that appear indistinguishable on standard benchmarks and reveal failure modes that are structurally interpretable rather than opaque. Beyond evaluation, our framework is contamination-free and supports the training and testing of reasoning models.

AI.tldr: X-RAY is a formal reasoning analysis system that uses calibrated probes to evaluate LLM reasoning capabilities through structural properties like constraint interaction and solution-space geometry, revealing systematic asymmetries in how models handle different types of reasoning challenges.
AI.motivation: Current LLM evaluations focus too much on task-level accuracy and conflate pattern matching with true reasoning capability, making it difficult to understand how LLMs actually reason and what their limitations are.
AI.method: X-RAY models reasoning capability as a function of extractable structure operationalized through formal properties. It generates probes via formal tools with controlled structural variations, using formal calibration and verification to isolate incremental structural information.
AI.result: Evaluation of state-of-the-art LLMs on math, physics, and chemistry problems revealed systematic asymmetry: models are robust to constraint refinement but degrade sharply under solution-space restructuring. Calibrated formal probes can differentiate models that appear identical on standard benchmarks and reveal structurally interpretable failure modes.
AI.conclusion: X-RAY provides an explainable reasoning analysis system that moves beyond superficial accuracy metrics to understand LLM reasoning through structural properties, offering a contamination-free framework for both evaluation and training of reasoning models.

title: Mixture of Universal Experts: Scaling Virtual Width via Depth-Width Transformation
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) decouples model capacity from per-token computation, yet their scalability remains limited by the physical dimensions of depth and width. To overcome this, we propose Mixture of Universal Experts (MOUE),a MoE generalization introducing a novel scaling dimension: Virtual Width. In general, MoUE aims to reuse a universal layer-agnostic expert pool across layers, converting depth into virtual width under a fixed per-token activation budget. However, two challenges remain: a routing path explosion from recursive expert reuse, and a mismatch between the exposure induced by reuse and the conventional load-balancing objectives. We address these with three core components: a Staggered Rotational Topology for structured expert sharing, a Universal Expert Load Balance for depth-aware exposure correction, and a Universal Router with lightweight trajectory state for coherent multi-step routing. Empirically, MoUE consistently outperforms matched MoE baselines by up to 1.3% across scaling regimes, enables progressive conversion of existing MoE checkpoints with up to 4.2% gains, and reveals a new scaling dimension for MoE architectures.

AI.tldr: MoUE introduces Virtual Width as a new scaling dimension for Mixture-of-Experts models by reusing a universal expert pool across layers, overcoming depth limitations while maintaining computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models are limited by physical constraints of depth and width scaling, requiring new architectural innovations to expand model capacity without increasing per-token computation costs.
AI.method: Proposes Mixture of Universal Experts (MoUE) with three core components: 1) Staggered Rotational Topology for structured expert sharing across layers, 2) Universal Expert Load Balance for depth-aware exposure correction, and 3) Universal Router with lightweight trajectory state for coherent multi-step routing.
AI.result: MoUE consistently outperforms matched MoE baselines by up to 1.3% across different scaling regimes, enables progressive conversion of existing MoE checkpoints with up to 4.2% performance gains, and successfully demonstrates a new scaling dimension for MoE architectures.
AI.conclusion: The MoUE framework successfully introduces Virtual Width as a novel scaling dimension for MoE models, providing a more efficient way to scale model capacity while maintaining computational efficiency, with practical benefits for both training from scratch and converting existing models.

title: STRUCTUREDAGENT: Planning with AND/OR Trees for Long-Horizon Web Tasks
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled agentic systems for sequential decision-making. Such agents must perceive their environment, reason across multiple time steps, and take actions that optimize long-term objectives. However, existing web agents struggle on complex, long-horizon tasks due to limited in-context memory for tracking history, weak planning abilities, and greedy behaviors that lead to premature termination. To address these challenges, we propose STRUCTUREDAGENT, a hierarchical planning framework with two core components: (1) an online hierarchical planner that uses dynamic AND/OR trees for efficient search and (2) a structured memory module that tracks and maintains candidate solutions to improve constraint satisfaction in information-seeking tasks. The framework also produces interpretable hierarchical plans, enabling easier debugging and facilitating human intervention when needed. Our results on WebVoyager, WebArena, and custom shopping benchmarks show that STRUCTUREDAGENT improves performance on long-horizon web-browsing tasks compared to standard LLM-based agents.

AI.tldr: STRUCTUREDAGENT is a hierarchical planning framework for web agents that addresses limitations in long-horizon tasks through AND/OR tree-based planning and structured memory, improving performance on complex web navigation benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing web agents struggle with complex, long-horizon tasks due to limited in-context memory for tracking history, weak planning abilities, and greedy behaviors that cause premature termination.
AI.method: Proposes STRUCTUREDAGENT with two core components: (1) an online hierarchical planner using dynamic AND/OR trees for efficient search, and (2) a structured memory module that tracks and maintains candidate solutions to improve constraint satisfaction.
AI.result: STRUCTUREDAGENT improves performance on long-horizon web-browsing tasks compared to standard LLM-based agents, as shown on WebVoyager, WebArena, and custom shopping benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The hierarchical planning framework with structured memory effectively addresses key limitations of existing web agents, enabling better performance on complex tasks while providing interpretable plans for debugging and human intervention.

title: Functionality-Oriented LLM Merging on the Fisher--Rao Manifold
summary:
Weight-space merging aims to combine multiple fine-tuned LLMs into a single model without retraining, yet most existing approaches remain fundamentally parameter-space heuristics. This creates three practical limitations. First, linear averaging, task vectors, and related rules operate on Euclidean coordinates, even though the desired goal is to merge functionality, i.e., predictive behaviors across tasks. Second, when the source checkpoints are farther apart or more heterogeneous, Euclidean blends often trigger representation collapse, manifested as activation variance shrinkage and effective-rank degradation, which sharply degrades accuracy. Third, many geometry-inspired methods are most natural for two-model interpolation and do not extend cleanly to merging N>2 experts with a principled objective.
  We address these issues by formulating model merging as computing a weighted Karcher mean on the Fisher--Rao manifold, which is locally equivalent to minimizing a KL-based function distance between predictive distributions. We derive a practical fixed-point algorithm using a lightweight spherical proxy that preserves norms and generalizes directly to multi-expert merging. Across various benchmarks and collapse diagnostics, our method remains stable as the number and heterogeneity of merged models increase, consistently outperforming prior baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a novel approach to model merging using Fisher-Rao geometry and Karcher means, addressing limitations of existing parameter-space heuristics by operating on predictive distributions rather than Euclidean coordinates.
AI.motivation: Current weight-space merging methods for LLMs face three key limitations: 1) They operate on Euclidean parameter coordinates rather than modeling functional/predictive behaviors, 2) Euclidean blends suffer from representation collapse when models are heterogeneous, and 3) Existing methods don't extend well to merging more than two models with a principled objective.
AI.method: The authors formulate model merging as computing a weighted Karcher mean on the Fisher-Rao manifold, which minimizes KL-based function distance between predictive distributions. They develop a practical fixed-point algorithm using a lightweight spherical proxy that preserves norms and generalizes to multi-expert merging.
AI.result: The proposed method demonstrates stability across various benchmarks and collapse diagnostics as the number and heterogeneity of merged models increase, consistently outperforming prior baselines in accuracy and preventing representation collapse.
AI.conclusion: By shifting from parameter-space averaging to functional merging via Fisher-Rao geometry, the approach provides a principled, scalable solution to model merging that handles heterogeneity better than existing methods.

title: WebChain: A Large-Scale Human-Annotated Dataset of Real-World Web Interaction Traces
summary: We introduce WebChain, the largest open-source dataset of human-annotated trajectories on real-world websites, designed to accelerate reproducible research in web agents. It contains 31,725 trajectories and 318k steps, featuring a core Triple Alignment of visual, structural, and action data to provide rich, multi-modal supervision. The data is collected via a scalable pipeline that ensures coverage of complex, high-value tasks often missed by synthetic methods. Leveraging this dataset, we propose a Dual Mid-Training recipe that decouples spatial grounding from planning, achieving state-of-the-art performance on our proposed WebChainBench and other public GUI benchmarks. Our work provides the data and insights necessary to build and rigorously evaluate the next generation of scalable web agents.

AI.tldr: WebChain is the largest open-source dataset of human-annotated web interaction trajectories with 31,725 trajectories and 318k steps, featuring triple alignment of visual, structural, and action data to accelerate reproducible research in web agents.
AI.motivation: Current web agent research lacks large-scale, high-quality human-annotated datasets with multi-modal supervision. Synthetic methods often miss complex, high-value tasks, limiting the development and rigorous evaluation of scalable web agents.
AI.method: Created WebChain through a scalable data collection pipeline capturing human interactions with real websites, featuring Triple Alignment of visual, structural, and action data. Proposed Dual Mid-Training recipe that decouples spatial grounding from planning for web agents.
AI.result: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on WebChainBench and other public GUI benchmarks. Provides the largest open-source web agent dataset with 31,725 trajectories and 318k steps, enabling better training and evaluation of web agents.
AI.conclusion: WebChain provides both the data and methodological insights needed to build and rigorously evaluate the next generation of scalable web agents, advancing reproducible research in web automation and agent development.

title: Lightweight and Scalable Transfer Learning Framework for Load Disaggregation
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Non-Intrusive Load Monitoring (NILM) aims to estimate appliance-level consumption from aggregate electrical signals recorded at a single measurement point. In recent years, the field has increasingly adopted deep learning approaches; however, cross-domain generalization remains a persistent challenge due to variations in appliance characteristics, usage patterns, and background loads across homes. Transfer learning provides a practical paradigm to adapt models with limited target data. However, existing methods often assume a fixed appliance set, lack flexibility for evolving real-world deployments, remain unsuitable for edge devices, or scale poorly for real-time operation. This paper proposes RefQuery, a scalable multi-appliance, multi-task NILM framework that conditions disaggregation on compact appliance fingerprints, allowing one shared model to serve many appliances without a fixed output set. RefQuery keeps a pretrained disaggregation network fully frozen and adapts to a target home by learning only a per-appliance embedding during a lightweight backpropagation stage. Experiments on three public datasets demonstrate that RefQuery delivers a strong accuracy-efficiency trade-off against single-appliance and multi-appliance baselines, including modern Transformer-based methods. These results support RefQuery as a practical path toward scalable, real-time NILM on resource-constrained edge devices.

AI.tldr: RefQuery is a scalable multi-appliance NILM framework that uses appliance fingerprints and frozen pretrained models to enable flexible, efficient disaggregation suitable for edge devices.
AI.motivation: Existing NILM methods struggle with cross-domain generalization due to appliance variations across homes. Current transfer learning approaches have limitations: fixed appliance sets, poor flexibility for real-world deployments, unsuitability for edge devices, and poor scalability for real-time operation.
AI.method: RefQuery proposes a multi-appliance, multi-task NILM framework that conditions disaggregation on compact appliance fingerprints. It keeps a pretrained disaggregation network fully frozen and adapts to target homes by learning only per-appliance embeddings during lightweight backpropagation, allowing one shared model to serve many appliances without a fixed output set.
AI.result: Experiments on three public datasets show RefQuery delivers strong accuracy-efficiency trade-off against both single-appliance and multi-appliance baselines, including modern Transformer-based methods.
AI.conclusion: RefQuery represents a practical path toward scalable, real-time NILM on resource-constrained edge devices, addressing key limitations of existing approaches in cross-domain generalization and deployment flexibility.

title: UniSTOK: Uniform Inductive Spatio-Temporal Kriging
summary: Spatio-temporal kriging aims to infer signals at unobserved locations from observed sensors and is critical to applications such as transportation and environmental monitoring. In practice, however, observed sensors themselves often exhibit heterogeneous missingness, forcing inductive kriging models to rely on crudely imputed inputs. This setting brings three key challenges: (1) it is unclear whether an value is a true signal or a missingness-induced artifact; (2) missingness is highly heterogeneous across sensors and time; (3) missing observations distort the local spatio-temporal structure. To address these issues, we propose Uniform Inductive Spatio-Temporal Kriging (UniSTOK), a plug-and-play framework that enhances existing inductive kriging backbones under missing observation. Our framework forms a dual-branch input consisting of the original observations and a jigsaw-augmented counterpart that synthesizes proxy signals only at missing entries. The two branches are then processed in parallel by a shared spatio-temporal backbone with explicit missingness mask modulation. Their outputs are finally adaptively fused via dual-channel attention. Experiments on multiple real-world datasets under diverse missing patterns demonstrate consistent and significant improvements.

AI.tldr: UniSTOK is a plug-and-play framework that improves existing inductive kriging models for spatio-temporal data by handling heterogeneous missing observations through dual-branch processing with jigsaw augmentation and mask modulation.
AI.motivation: Spatio-temporal kriging is crucial for applications like transportation and environmental monitoring, but real-world sensor data often suffers from heterogeneous missingness. This creates three main problems: difficulty distinguishing true signals from missingness artifacts, highly variable missingness patterns across sensors and time, and distortion of local spatio-temporal structures when using crude imputation methods.
AI.method: Proposes Uniform Inductive Spatio-Temporal Kriging (UniSTOK) with: (1) dual-branch input - original observations plus jigsaw-augmented counterpart that synthesizes proxy signals only at missing entries; (2) shared spatio-temporal backbone with explicit missingness mask modulation; (3) adaptive fusion via dual-channel attention.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple real-world datasets under diverse missing patterns show consistent and significant improvements over existing methods.
AI.conclusion: UniSTOK effectively addresses the challenges of heterogeneous missingness in spatio-temporal kriging by providing a plug-and-play framework that enhances existing inductive kriging backbones without requiring architecture changes, making it practical for real-world applications with imperfect sensor data.

title: Building AI Coding Agents for the Terminal: Scaffolding, Harness, Context Engineering, and Lessons Learned
comment: Work in progress, new versions will be updated continuously
summary: The landscape of AI coding assistance is undergoing a fundamental shift from complex IDE plugins to versatile, terminal-native agents. Operating directly where developers manage source control, execute builds, and deploy environments, CLI-based agents offer unprecedented autonomy for long-horizon development tasks. In this paper, we present OPENDEV, an open-source, command-line coding agent engineered specifically for this new paradigm. Effective autonomous assistance requires strict safety controls and highly efficient context management to prevent context bloat and reasoning degradation. OPENDEV overcomes these challenges through a compound AI system architecture with workload-specialized model routing, a dual-agent architecture separating planning from execution, lazy tool discovery, and adaptive context compaction that progressively reduces older observations. Furthermore, it employs an automated memory system to accumulate project-specific knowledge across sessions and counteracts instruction fade-out through event-driven system reminders. By enforcing explicit reasoning phases and prioritizing context efficiency, OPENDEV provides a secure, extensible foundation for terminal-first AI assistance, offering a blueprint for robust autonomous software engineering.

AI.tldr: OPENDEV is an open-source CLI-based coding agent that uses a compound AI system with specialized model routing, dual-agent architecture, lazy tool discovery, and adaptive context compaction to provide secure, efficient terminal-first AI assistance for long-horizon development tasks.
AI.motivation: The AI coding assistance landscape is shifting from complex IDE plugins to terminal-native agents that operate where developers actually work (managing source control, executing builds, deploying environments). CLI-based agents offer unprecedented autonomy for long-horizon development tasks but face challenges with safety controls, context management, and preventing context bloat and reasoning degradation.
AI.method: OPENDEV employs a compound AI system architecture featuring: 1) Workload-specialized model routing, 2) Dual-agent architecture separating planning from execution, 3) Lazy tool discovery, 4) Adaptive context compaction that progressively reduces older observations, 5) Automated memory system to accumulate project-specific knowledge across sessions, and 6) Event-driven system reminders to counteract instruction fade-out.
AI.result: OPENDEV provides a secure, extensible foundation for terminal-first AI assistance by enforcing explicit reasoning phases and prioritizing context efficiency, overcoming the challenges of safety controls and context management in CLI-based coding agents.
AI.conclusion: OPENDEV offers a blueprint for robust autonomous software engineering, demonstrating how a well-architected CLI-based coding agent can provide effective, safe assistance for long-horizon development tasks while operating directly in developers' natural workflow environment.

title: Non-Euclidean Gradient Descent Operates at the Edge of Stability
summary: The Edge of Stability (EoS) is a phenomenon where the sharpness (largest eigenvalue) of the Hessian converges to $2/η$ during training with gradient descent (GD) with a step-size $η$. Despite (apparently) violating classical smoothness assumptions, EoS has been widely observed in deep learning, but its theoretical foundations remain incomplete. We provide an interpretation of EoS through the lens of Directional Smoothness Mishkin et al. [2024]. This interpretation naturally extends to non-Euclidean norms, which we use to define generalized sharpness under an arbitrary norm. Our generalized sharpness measure includes previously studied vanilla GD and preconditioned GD as special cases, as well as methods for which EoS has not been studied, such as $\ell_{\infty}$-descent, Block CD, Spectral GD, and Muon without momentum. Through experiments on neural networks, we show that non-Euclidean GD with our generalized sharpness also exhibits progressive sharpening followed by oscillations around or above the threshold $2/η$. Practically, our framework provides a single, geometry-aware spectral measure that works across optimizers.

AI.tldr: The paper extends the Edge of Stability (EoS) phenomenon to non-Euclidean norms through Directional Smoothness, providing a unified framework that explains EoS across various optimization methods including vanilla GD, preconditioned GD, ℓ∞-descent, Block CD, Spectral GD, and Muon without momentum.
AI.motivation: The Edge of Stability phenomenon is widely observed in deep learning but violates classical smoothness assumptions and lacks complete theoretical foundations. The authors aim to provide a more comprehensive theoretical interpretation of EoS that works across different optimization methods and geometries.
AI.method: The authors interpret EoS through Directional Smoothness and extend it to non-Euclidean norms to define a generalized sharpness measure under arbitrary norms. This framework includes various optimization methods as special cases, and they experimentally validate it on neural networks.
AI.result: The generalized sharpness framework successfully explains EoS across multiple optimization methods, showing that non-Euclidean GD with their generalized sharpness exhibits the same progressive sharpening followed by oscillations around the threshold 2/η. The framework provides a single geometry-aware spectral measure that works across different optimizers.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a unified theoretical framework for understanding the Edge of Stability phenomenon across diverse optimization methods and geometries, bridging the gap between empirical observations and theoretical understanding in deep learning optimization.

title: Ailed: A Psyche-Driven Chess Engine with Dynamic Emotional Modulation
comment: 27 pages, 8 figures, 11 tables. Open source: https://github.com/chrnx-dev/ailed-chess
summary:
Chess engines passed human strength years ago, but they still don't play like humans. A grandmaster under clock pressure blunders in ways a club player on a hot streak never would. Conventional engines capture none of this.
  This paper proposes a personality x psyche decomposition to produce behavioral variability in chess play, drawing on patterns observed in human games. Personality is static -- a preset that pins down the engine's character. Psyche is dynamic -- a bounded scalar ψ_t \in [-100, +100], recomputed from five positional factors after every move. These two components feed into an audio-inspired signal chain (noise gate, compressor/expander, five-band equalizer, saturation limiter) that reshapes move probability distributions on the fly. The chain doesn't care what engine sits behind it: any system that outputs move probabilities will do. It needs no search and carries no state beyond ψ_t.
  I test the framework across 12,414 games against Maia2-1100, feeding it two probability sources that differ by ~2,800x in training data. Both show the same monotonic gradient in top-move agreement (~20-25 pp spread from stress to overconfidence), which tells us the behavioral variation comes from the signal chain, not from the model underneath. When the psyche runs overconfident, the chain mostly gets out of the way (66% agreement with vanilla Maia2). Under stress, the competitive score falls from 50.8% to 30.1%. The patterns are reminiscent of tilt and overconfidence as described in human play, but I should be upfront: this study includes no human-subject validation.

AI.tldr: A framework that adds human-like behavioral variability to chess engines using personality (static) and psyche (dynamic) components that reshape move probabilities through an audio-inspired signal chain, producing tilt and overconfidence patterns without search or human validation.
AI.motivation: Chess engines don't play like humans despite surpassing human strength. Human chess involves psychological factors like blundering under pressure and overconfidence that conventional engines don't capture. The paper aims to produce behavioral variability reminiscent of human psychological patterns in chess play.
AI.method: Uses personality (static preset) and psyche (dynamic scalar ψ_t ∈ [-100,+100] recomputed from five positional factors) to feed into an audio-inspired signal chain (noise gate, compressor/expander, five-band equalizer, saturation limiter) that reshapes move probability distributions. The chain works with any engine outputting move probabilities, requires no search, and carries no state beyond ψ_t.
AI.result: Tested across 12,414 games against Maia2-1100 with two different probability sources (2,800x training data difference). Both showed same monotonic gradient in top-move agreement (20-25 percentage point spread from stress to overconfidence), confirming behavioral variation comes from signal chain. Overconfident psyche: 66% agreement with vanilla Maia2. Stressed psyche: competitive score dropped from 50.8% to 30.1%, producing patterns reminiscent of human tilt and overconfidence.
AI.conclusion: The personality x psyche decomposition with audio-inspired signal chain successfully produces human-like behavioral variability in chess engines without requiring search or engine modifications. The approach generates psychologically plausible patterns (tilt, overconfidence) that conventional engines lack, though the study acknowledges lack of human-subject validation. The method works independently of the underlying chess engine.

title: Poisoning the Inner Prediction Logic of Graph Neural Networks for Clean-Label Backdoor Attacks
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have achieved remarkable results in various tasks. Recent studies reveal that graph backdoor attacks can poison the GNN model to predict test nodes with triggers attached as the target class. However, apart from injecting triggers to training nodes, these graph backdoor attacks generally require altering the labels of trigger-attached training nodes into the target class, which is impractical in real-world scenarios. In this work, we focus on the clean-label graph backdoor attack, a realistic but understudied topic where training labels are not modifiable. According to our preliminary analysis, existing graph backdoor attacks generally fail under the clean-label setting. Our further analysis identifies that the core failure of existing methods lies in their inability to poison the prediction logic of GNN models, leading to the triggers being deemed unimportant for prediction. Therefore, we study a novel problem of effective clean-label graph backdoor attacks by poisoning the inner prediction logic of GNN models. We propose BA-Logic to solve the problem by coordinating a poisoned node selector and a logic-poisoning trigger generator. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that our method effectively enhances the attack success rate and surpasses state-of-the-art graph backdoor attack competitors under clean-label settings. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/BA-Logic

AI.tldr: BA-Logic is a novel clean-label graph backdoor attack method that coordinates a poisoned node selector and logic-poisoning trigger generator to poison GNN models' prediction logic, achieving effective attacks without modifying training labels.
AI.motivation: Existing graph backdoor attacks require modifying training labels (dirty-label), which is impractical in real-world scenarios. Clean-label attacks (where training labels are not modifiable) are realistic but understudied, and existing methods fail in this setting because they cannot poison the prediction logic of GNN models.
AI.method: Proposes BA-Logic with two coordinated components: 1) A poisoned node selector to identify optimal training nodes for trigger injection, and 2) A logic-poisoning trigger generator that creates triggers designed to manipulate the inner prediction logic of GNN models rather than just adding superficial patterns.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real-world datasets show BA-Logic significantly enhances attack success rate and outperforms state-of-the-art graph backdoor attack methods under clean-label settings, demonstrating the effectiveness of poisoning GNN prediction logic.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully addresses the realistic clean-label graph backdoor attack problem by focusing on poisoning the prediction logic of GNN models through coordinated node selection and trigger generation, opening new directions for studying realistic backdoor attacks in graph learning.

title: PACE: A Personalized Adaptive Curriculum Engine for 9-1-1 Call-taker Training
summary: 9-1-1 call-taking training requires mastery of over a thousand interdependent skills, covering diverse incident types and protocol-specific nuances. A nationwide labor shortage is already straining training capacity, but effective instruction still demands that trainers tailor objectives to each trainee's evolving competencies. This personalization burden is one that current practice cannot scale. Partnering with Metro Nashville Department of Emergency Communications (MNDEC), we propose PACE (Personalized Adaptive Curriculum Engine), a co-pilot system that augments trainer decision-making by (1) maintaining probabilistic beliefs over trainee skill states, (2) modeling individual learning and forgetting dynamics, and (3) recommending training scenarios that balance acquisition of new competencies with retention of existing ones. PACE propagates evidence over a structured skill graph to accelerate diagnostic coverage and applies contextual bandits to select scenarios that target gaps the trainee is prepared to address. Empirical results show that PACE achieves 19.50% faster time-to-competence and 10.95% higher terminal mastery compared to state-of-the-art frameworks. Co-pilot studies with practicing training officers further demonstrate a 95.45% alignment rate between PACE's and experts' pedagogical judgments on real-world cases. Under estimation, PACE cuts turnaround time to merely 34 seconds from 11.58 minutes, up to 95.08% reduction.

AI.tldr: PACE is an AI-powered training co-pilot that personalizes 9-1-1 call-taker training by modeling individual skill states and recommending optimal training scenarios, achieving significantly faster competence development and higher mastery compared to existing methods.
AI.motivation: 9-1-1 call-taking requires mastery of over a thousand interdependent skills, creating complex training challenges. A nationwide labor shortage strains training capacity, while effective instruction demands personalization to each trainee's evolving competencies - a burden current practices cannot scale.
AI.method: PACE maintains probabilistic beliefs over trainee skill states using Bayesian inference over a structured skill graph. It models individual learning and forgetting dynamics, then applies contextual bandits to recommend training scenarios that balance new skill acquisition with existing skill retention, accelerating diagnostic coverage.
AI.result: PACE achieves 19.50% faster time-to-competence and 10.95% higher terminal mastery compared to state-of-the-art frameworks. In co-pilot studies, PACE shows 95.45% alignment with expert pedagogical judgments. It reduces scenario selection turnaround time from 11.58 minutes to just 34 seconds (95.08% reduction).
AI.conclusion: PACE successfully augments trainer decision-making through AI-driven personalization, dramatically improving training efficiency and effectiveness for 9-1-1 call-takers while maintaining strong alignment with expert judgment, addressing critical scalability challenges in emergency communications training.

title: MCEL: Margin-Based Cross-Entropy Loss for Error-Tolerant Quantized Neural Networks
summary: Robustness to bit errors is a key requirement for the reliable use of neural networks (NNs) on emerging approximate computing platforms and error-prone memory technologies. A common approach to achieve bit error tolerance in NNs is injecting bit flips during training according to a predefined error model. While effective in certain scenarios, training-time bit flip injection introduces substantial computational overhead, often degrades inference accuracy at high error rates, and scales poorly for larger NN architectures. These limitations make error injection an increasingly impractical solution for ensuring robustness on future approximate computing platforms and error-prone memory technologies. In this work, we investigate the mechanisms that enable NNs to tolerate bit errors without relying on error-aware training. We establish a direct connection between bit error tolerance and classification margins at the output layer. Building on this insight, we propose a novel loss function, the Margin Cross-Entropy Loss (MCEL), which explicitly promotes logit-level margin separation while preserving the favorable optimization properties of the standard cross-entropy loss. Furthermore, MCEL introduces an interpretable margin parameter that allows robustness to be tuned in a principled manner. Extensive experimental evaluations across multiple datasets of varying complexity, diverse NN architectures, and a range of quantization schemes demonstrate that MCEL substantially improves bit error tolerance, up to 15 % in accuracy for an error rate of 1 %. Our proposed MCEL method is simple to implement, efficient, and can be integrated as a drop-in replacement for standard CEL. It provides a scalable and principled alternative to training-time bit flip injection, offering new insights into the origins of NN robustness and enabling more efficient deployment on approximate computing and memory systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Margin Cross-Entropy Loss (MCEL), a novel loss function that improves neural network robustness to bit errors without computationally expensive error injection during training, by promoting logit-level margin separation at the output layer.
AI.motivation: Bit error robustness is crucial for neural networks deployed on approximate computing platforms and error-prone memory technologies. Existing approaches using bit flip injection during training have substantial drawbacks: high computational overhead, accuracy degradation at high error rates, and poor scalability for large architectures.
AI.method: The authors establish a connection between bit error tolerance and classification margins at the output layer. Based on this insight, they propose Margin Cross-Entropy Loss (MCEL), which explicitly promotes logit-level margin separation while maintaining the optimization properties of standard cross-entropy loss. MCEL includes an interpretable margin parameter for principled robustness tuning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple datasets, diverse neural network architectures, and various quantization schemes show that MCEL substantially improves bit error tolerance - up to 15% accuracy improvement for 1% error rate. The method is simple to implement, efficient, and can serve as a drop-in replacement for standard cross-entropy loss.
AI.conclusion: MCEL provides a scalable and principled alternative to training-time bit flip injection for achieving bit error robustness in neural networks. It offers new insights into the origins of neural network robustness and enables more efficient deployment on approximate computing and memory systems.

title: Legal interpretation and AI: from expert systems to argumentation and LLMs
summary: AI and Law research has encountered legal interpretation in different ways, in the context of its evolving approaches and methodologies. Research on expert system has focused on legal knowledge engineering, with the goal of ensuring that human-generated interpretations can be precisely transferred into knowledge-bases, to be consistently applied. Research on argumentation has aimed at representing the structure of interpretive arguments, as well as their dialectical interactions, to assess of the acceptability of interpretive claims within argumentation frameworks. Research on machine learning has focused on the automated generation of interpretive suggestions and arguments, through general and specialised language models, now being increasingly deployed in legal practice.

AI.tldr: AI and Law research has evolved through three main approaches to legal interpretation: expert systems for precise knowledge engineering, argumentation frameworks for assessing interpretive claims, and machine learning for generating interpretive suggestions using language models.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to explore how AI and Law research has addressed the complex problem of legal interpretation through different methodological approaches over time, acknowledging the evolving nature of the field and the need to understand how various AI techniques have been applied to this fundamental legal task.
AI.method: The paper analyzes three distinct methodological approaches: 1) Expert systems focusing on legal knowledge engineering to transfer human interpretations into knowledge bases, 2) Argumentation research representing the structure of interpretive arguments and their dialectical interactions within argumentation frameworks, and 3) Machine learning approaches using general and specialized language models for automated generation of interpretive suggestions and arguments.
AI.result: The research shows that AI and Law has approached legal interpretation through different paradigms: expert systems aim for precision and consistency in applying human interpretations, argumentation frameworks enable assessment of interpretive claims through structured argumentation, and machine learning techniques are increasingly being deployed in legal practice for automated interpretive generation.
AI.conclusion: AI and Law research has systematically engaged with legal interpretation through evolving methodologies, moving from knowledge engineering to argumentation frameworks and now to machine learning approaches, with the latter becoming increasingly practical for real-world legal applications.

title: Asymptotic Behavior of Multi--Task Learning: Implicit Regularization and Double Descent Effects
summary: Multi--task learning seeks to improve the generalization error by leveraging the common information shared by multiple related tasks. One challenge in multi--task learning is identifying formulations capable of uncovering the common information shared between different but related tasks. This paper provides a precise asymptotic analysis of a popular multi--task formulation associated with misspecified perceptron learning models. The main contribution of this paper is to precisely determine the reasons behind the benefits gained from combining multiple related tasks. Specifically, we show that combining multiple tasks is asymptotically equivalent to a traditional formulation with additional regularization terms that help improve the generalization performance. Another contribution is to empirically study the impact of combining tasks on the generalization error. In particular, we empirically show that the combination of multiple tasks postpones the double descent phenomenon and can mitigate it asymptotically.

AI.tldr: A theoretical analysis of multi-task learning shows that combining related tasks provides regularization benefits that improve generalization and can postpone/mitigate the double descent phenomenon.
AI.motivation: Multi-task learning aims to improve generalization by leveraging common information across related tasks, but it's challenging to identify formulations that can effectively uncover this shared information, especially in misspecified models.
AI.method: The paper provides a precise asymptotic analysis of a popular multi-task formulation associated with misspecified perceptron learning models, examining the theoretical benefits of task combination.
AI.result: Combining multiple tasks is asymptotically equivalent to a traditional formulation with additional regularization terms that improve generalization. Empirically, multi-task learning postpones the double descent phenomenon and can mitigate it asymptotically.
AI.conclusion: Multi-task learning provides both theoretical and practical benefits through regularization effects, explaining why combining related tasks improves generalization performance and helps address issues like double descent.

title: Judge Reliability Harness: Stress Testing the Reliability of LLM Judges
comment: Accepted at Agents in the Wild: Safety, Security, and Beyond Workshop at ICLR 2026 - April 26, 2026, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil
summary: We present the Judge Reliability Harness, an open source library for constructing validation suites that test the reliability of LLM judges. As LLM based scoring is widely deployed in AI benchmarks, more tooling is needed to efficiently assess the reliability of these methods. Given a benchmark dataset and an LLM judge configuration, the harness generates reliability tests that evaluate both binary judgment accuracy and ordinal grading performance for free-response and agentic task formats. We evaluate four state-of-the-art judges across four benchmarks spanning safety, persuasion, misuse, and agentic behavior, and find meaningful variation in performance across models and perturbation types, highlighting opportunities to improve the robustness of LLM judges. No judge that we evaluated is uniformly reliable across benchmarks using our harness. For example, our preliminary experiments on judges revealed consistency issues as measured by accuracy in judging another LLM's ability to complete a task due to simple text formatting changes, paraphrasing, changes in verbosity, and flipping the ground truth label in LLM-produced responses. The code for this tool is available at: https://github.com/RANDCorporation/judge-reliability-harness

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Judge Reliability Harness, an open-source library for testing the reliability of LLM judges used in AI benchmarks, revealing that no current judge is uniformly reliable across different benchmarks.
AI.motivation: With LLM-based scoring being widely deployed in AI benchmarks, there's a need for tooling to efficiently assess the reliability of these judging methods. Current judges may have undiscovered reliability issues.
AI.method: The harness generates reliability tests that evaluate both binary judgment accuracy and ordinal grading performance for free-response and agentic task formats. It tests judges across four benchmarks spanning safety, persuasion, misuse, and agentic behavior.
AI.result: Evaluation of four state-of-the-art judges showed meaningful variation in performance across models and perturbation types. No evaluated judge was uniformly reliable across benchmarks. Judges exhibited consistency issues due to simple text formatting changes, paraphrasing, verbosity changes, and flipped ground truth labels.
AI.conclusion: The Judge Reliability Harness highlights opportunities to improve LLM judge robustness. The tool is open-source and available for the community to use in assessing judge reliability, with findings showing current judges need significant improvement in consistency and reliability.

title: Deep Learning-Driven Friendly Jamming for Secure Multicarrier ISAC Under Channel Uncertainty
summary: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems promise efficient spectrum utilization by jointly supporting radar sensing and wireless communication. This paper presents a deep learning-driven framework for enhancing physical-layer security in multicarrier ISAC systems under imperfect channel state information (CSI) and in the presence of unknown eavesdropper (Eve) locations. Unlike conventional ISAC-based friendly jamming (FJ) approaches that require Eve's CSI or precise angle-of-arrival (AoA) estimates, our method exploits radar echo feedback to guide directional jamming without explicit Eve's information. To enhance robustness to radar sensing uncertainty, we propose a radar-aware neural network that jointly optimizes beamforming and jamming by integrating a novel nonparametric Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) estimator based on f-divergence. The jamming design satisfies the Cramer-Rao lower bound (CRLB) constraints even in the presence of noisy AoA. For efficient implementation, we introduce a quantized tensor train-based encoder that reduces the model size by more than 100 times with negligible performance loss. We also integrate a non-overlapping secure scheme into the proposed framework, in which specific sub-bands can be dedicated solely to communication. Extensive simulations demonstrate that the proposed solution achieves significant improvements in secrecy rate, reduced block error rate (BLER), and strong robustness against CSI uncertainty and angular estimation errors, underscoring the effectiveness of the proposed deep learning-driven friendly jamming framework under practical ISAC impairments.

AI.tldr: A deep learning framework that uses radar echo feedback to enable directional friendly jamming for physical-layer security in ISAC systems without requiring eavesdropper CSI or precise location information, while addressing sensing uncertainties through neural network optimization.
AI.motivation: Current ISAC-based friendly jamming approaches require eavesdropper's CSI or precise AoA estimates, which are impractical in real scenarios with imperfect information and unknown eavesdropper locations. There's a need for robust security enhancement under practical impairments.
AI.method: 1) Uses radar echo feedback to guide directional jamming without explicit Eve information 2) Proposes radar-aware neural network jointly optimizing beamforming and jamming 3) Integrates nonparametric FIM estimator based on f-divergence for robustness 4) Employs quantized tensor train-based encoder for 100x model compression 5) Includes non-overlapping secure scheme with dedicated communication sub-bands.
AI.result: Significant improvements in secrecy rate, reduced BLER, strong robustness against CSI uncertainty and angular estimation errors. The quantized encoder reduces model size by over 100 times with negligible performance loss.
AI.conclusion: The proposed deep learning-driven framework effectively enhances physical-layer security in practical ISAC systems by leveraging radar sensing for friendly jamming without requiring eavesdropper information, demonstrating robustness against practical impairments through neural network optimization and efficient implementation techniques.

title: Dissociating Direct Access from Inference in AI Introspection
summary: Introspection is a foundational cognitive ability, but its mechanism is not well understood. Recent work has shown that AI models can introspect. We study their mechanism of introspection, first extensively replicating Lindsey et al. (2025)'s thought injection detection paradigm in large open-source models. We show that these models detect injected representations via two separable mechanisms: (i) probability-matching (inferring from perceived anomaly of the prompt) and (ii) direct access to internal states. The direct access mechanism is content-agnostic: models detect that an anomaly occurred but cannot reliably identify its semantic content. The two model classes we study confabulate injected concepts that are high-frequency and concrete (e.g., "apple'"); for them correct concept guesses typically require significantly more tokens. This content-agnostic introspective mechanism is consistent with leading theories in philosophy and psychology.

AI.tldr: AI models can detect injected thoughts via two mechanisms: probability-matching (inferring from prompt anomalies) and direct access to internal states, but the direct access is content-agnostic - models know something is wrong but can't reliably identify what.
AI.motivation: Introspection is a foundational cognitive ability whose mechanisms are not well understood. Recent findings show AI models can introspect, but how they accomplish this remains unclear.
AI.method: Extensive replication of Lindsey et al. (2025)'s thought injection detection paradigm in large open-source models, studying how models detect injected representations.
AI.result: Models detect injected representations via two separable mechanisms: 1) probability-matching (inferring from perceived prompt anomaly), and 2) direct access to internal states (which is content-agnostic). Models confabulate high-frequency concrete concepts and need significantly more tokens for correct concept guesses.
AI.conclusion: The content-agnostic introspective mechanism found in AI models aligns with leading theories in philosophy and psychology about human introspection mechanisms.

title: Reward-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning
summary: RL agents are typically trained under a single, fixed reward function, which makes them brittle to reward misspecification and limits their ability to adapt to changing task preferences. We introduce Reward-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning (RCRL), a framework that trains a single agent to optimize a family of reward specifications while collecting experience under only one nominal objective. RCRL conditions the agent on reward parameterizations and learns multiple reward objectives from a shared replay data entirely off-policy, enabling a single policy to represent reward-specific behaviors. Across single-task, multi-task, and vision-based benchmarks, we show that RCRL not only improves performance under the nominal reward parameterization, but also enables efficient adaptation to new parameterizations. Our results demonstrate that RCRL provides a scalable mechanism for learning robust, steerable policies without sacrificing the simplicity of single-task training.

AI.tldr: RCRL trains a single RL agent to optimize multiple reward functions while collecting experience under only one nominal objective, enabling reward-conditional policies that can adapt to different reward specifications without additional training.
AI.motivation: Standard RL agents are brittle to reward misspecification and cannot adapt to changing task preferences, as they are trained under a single fixed reward function. This limits their robustness and flexibility in real-world applications.
AI.method: Reward-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning (RCRL) conditions agents on reward parameterizations and learns multiple reward objectives from shared replay data entirely off-policy. The framework trains a single policy to represent reward-specific behaviors while collecting experience under only one nominal objective.
AI.result: RCRL improves performance under the nominal reward parameterization and enables efficient adaptation to new reward parameterizations. Results across single-task, multi-task, and vision-based benchmarks show that RCRL learns robust, steerable policies.
AI.conclusion: RCRL provides a scalable mechanism for learning robust and steerable policies without sacrificing the simplicity of single-task training, addressing the brittleness of traditional RL to reward misspecification.

title: Distributed Partial Information Puzzles: Examining Common Ground Construction Under Epistemic Asymmetry
summary: Establishing common ground, a shared set of beliefs and mutually recognized facts, is fundamental to collaboration, yet remains a challenge for current AI systems, especially in multimodal, multiparty settings, where the collaborators bring different information to the table. We introduce the Distributed Partial Information Puzzle (DPIP), a collaborative construction task that elicits rich multimodal communication under epistemic asymmetry. We present a multimodal dataset of these interactions, annotated and temporally aligned across speech, gesture, and action modalities to support reasoning over propositional content and belief dynamics. We then evaluate two paradigms for modeling common ground (CG): (1) state-of-the-art large language models (LLMs), prompted to infer shared beliefs from multimodal updates, and (2) an axiomatic pipeline grounded in Dynamic Epistemic Logic (DEL) that incrementally performs the same task. Results on the annotated DPIP data indicate that it poses a challenge to modern LLMs' abilities to track both task progression and belief state.

AI.tldr: A multimodal collaborative task (DPIP) challenges AI systems to track beliefs and build common ground across speech, gesture, and action, testing LLMs against logic-based approaches.
AI.motivation: Current AI systems struggle to establish common ground (shared beliefs) in multimodal, multiparty settings where collaborators have different information, which is fundamental for effective collaboration.
AI.method: Introduced the Distributed Partial Information Puzzle (DPIP) - a collaborative construction task that elicits rich multimodal communication. Collected and annotated a multimodal dataset with temporal alignment across speech, gesture, and action modalities. Evaluated two approaches: (1) state-of-the-art LLMs prompted to infer shared beliefs, and (2) an axiomatic pipeline based on Dynamic Epistemic Logic (DEL).
AI.result: The DPIP task poses a significant challenge to modern LLMs' abilities to track both task progression and belief state. The dataset supports reasoning over propositional content and belief dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Building common ground in multimodal, multiparty collaborative settings remains challenging for current AI systems, and the DPIP task and dataset provide a valuable benchmark for evaluating approaches to belief tracking and shared understanding.

title: Synchronization-based clustering on the unit hypersphere
summary: Clustering on the unit hypersphere is a fundamental problem in various fields, with applications ranging from gene expression analysis to text and image classification. Traditional clustering methods are not always suitable for unit sphere data, as they do not account for the geometric structure of the sphere. We introduce a novel algorithm for clustering data represented as points on the unit sphere $\mathbf{S}^{d-1}$. Our method is based on the $d$-dimensional generalized Kuramoto model. The effectiveness of the introduced method is demonstrated on synthetic and real-world datasets. Results are compared with some of the traditional clustering methods, showing that our method achieves similar or better results in terms of clustering accuracy.

AI.tldr: Novel clustering algorithm for unit sphere data using generalized Kuramoto model, showing competitive performance vs traditional methods.
AI.motivation: Traditional clustering methods are not suitable for unit hypersphere data because they don't account for the geometric structure of the sphere, while such data appears in many applications like gene expression analysis, text and image classification.
AI.method: Introduces a novel clustering algorithm based on the d-dimensional generalized Kuramoto model for clustering data represented as points on the unit sphere S^{d-1}.
AI.result: Demonstrated effectiveness on synthetic and real-world datasets, achieving similar or better clustering accuracy compared to traditional clustering methods.
AI.conclusion: The generalized Kuramoto model provides an effective framework for clustering unit sphere data, overcoming limitations of traditional methods that ignore the spherical geometry.

title: Towards Provably Unbiased LLM Judges via Bias-Bounded Evaluation
summary: As AI models progress beyond simple chatbots into more complex workflows, we draw ever closer to the event horizon beyond which AI systems will be utilized in autonomous, self-maintaining feedback loops. Any autonomous AI system will depend on automated, verifiable rewards and feedback; in settings where ground truth is sparse or non-deterministic, one practical source of such rewards is an LLM-as-a-Judge. Although LLM judges continue to improve, the literature has yet to introduce systems capable of enforcing standards with strong guarantees, particularly when bias vectors are unknown or adversarially discovered. To remedy this issue, we propose average bias-boundedness (A-BB), an algorithmic framework which formally guarantees reductions of harm/impact as a result of any measurable bias in an LLM judge. Evaluating on Arena-Hard-Auto with four LLM judges, we achieve (tau=0.5, delta=0.01) bias-bounded guarantees while retaining 61-99% correlation with original rankings across formatting and schematic bias settings, with most judge-bias combinations exceeding 80%. The code to reproduce our findings is available at https://github.com/penfever/bias-bounded-evaluation.

AI.tldr: Proposes Average Bias-Boundedness (A-BB) framework that provides formal guarantees to reduce harm from any measurable bias in LLM judges used for AI system rewards, achieving strong bias-bounded guarantees while maintaining high correlation with original rankings.
AI.motivation: As AI systems move toward autonomous, self-maintaining feedback loops, they need automated, verifiable rewards. LLM-as-a-Judge provides practical rewards in settings with sparse ground truth, but current systems lack strong guarantees against unknown or adversarial bias.
AI.method: Proposes Average Bias-Boundedness (A-BB), an algorithmic framework that formally guarantees reductions of harm/impact from any measurable bias in LLM judges. Evaluated on Arena-Hard-Auto dataset with four LLM judges under different bias settings.
AI.result: Achieved (tau=0.5, delta=0.01) bias-bounded guarantees while retaining 61-99% correlation with original rankings across formatting and schematic bias settings, with most judge-bias combinations exceeding 80% correlation.
AI.conclusion: A-BB provides a practical solution for ensuring bias-bounded guarantees in LLM judges, enabling safer deployment of autonomous AI systems that rely on automated rewards from LLM judges, particularly in adversarial scenarios.

title: Aura: Universal Multi-dimensional Exogenous Integration for Aviation Time Series
summary: Time series forecasting has witnessed an increasing demand across diverse industrial applications, where accurate predictions are pivotal for informed decision-making. Beyond numerical time series data, reliable forecasting in practical scenarios requires integrating diverse exogenous factors. Such exogenous information is often multi-dimensional or even multimodal, introducing heterogeneous interactions that unimodal time series models struggle to capture. In this paper, we delve into an aviation maintenance scenario and identify three distinct types of exogenous factors that influence temporal dynamics through distinct interaction modes. Based on this empirical insight, we propose Aura, a universal framework that explicitly organizes and encodes heterogeneous external information according to its interaction mode with the target time series. Specifically, Aura utilizes a tailored tripartite encoding mechanism to embed heterogeneous features into well-established time series models, ensuring seamless integration of non-sequential context. Extensive experiments on a large-scale, three-year industrial dataset from China Southern Airlines, covering the Boeing 777 and Airbus A320 fleets, demonstrate that Aura consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance across all baselines and exhibits superior adaptability. Our findings highlight Aura's potential as a general-purpose enhancement for aviation safety and reliability.

AI.tldr: Aura is a universal framework for time series forecasting that explicitly organizes and encodes heterogeneous exogenous factors based on their distinct interaction modes with target time series, demonstrating superior performance on large-scale aviation maintenance data.
AI.motivation: Practical time series forecasting requires integrating diverse exogenous factors (multi-dimensional or multimodal) beyond just numerical data. Real-world scenarios like aviation maintenance involve heterogeneous external information that unimodal time series models struggle to capture, creating a need for better integration methods.
AI.method: The proposed Aura framework explicitly organizes and encodes heterogeneous external information according to its interaction mode with target time series. It uses a tailored tripartite encoding mechanism to embed heterogeneous features into well-established time series models, enabling seamless integration of non-sequential context.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on a large-scale, three-year industrial dataset from China Southern Airlines (covering Boeing 777 and Airbus A320 fleets) show that Aura consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance across all baselines and exhibits superior adaptability.
AI.conclusion: Aura demonstrates potential as a general-purpose enhancement for aviation safety and reliability by effectively integrating heterogeneous exogenous factors into time series forecasting, addressing the limitations of existing unimodal approaches in real-world industrial applications.

title: The Spike, the Sparse and the Sink: Anatomy of Massive Activations and Attention Sinks
summary: We study two recurring phenomena in Transformer language models: massive activations, in which a small number of tokens exhibit extreme outliers in a few channels, and attention sinks, in which certain tokens attract disproportionate attention mass regardless of semantic relevance. Prior work observes that these phenomena frequently co-occur and often involve the same tokens, but their functional roles and causal relationship remain unclear. Through systematic experiments, we show that the co-occurrence is largely an architectural artifact of modern Transformer design, and that the two phenomena serve related but distinct functions. Massive activations operate globally: they induce near-constant hidden representations that persist across layers, effectively functioning as implicit parameters of the model. Attention sinks operate locally: they modulate attention outputs across heads and bias individual heads toward short-range dependencies. We identify the pre-norm configuration as the key choice that enables the co-occurrence, and show that ablating it causes the two phenomena to decouple.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates two co-occurring phenomena in Transformer LMs - massive activations and attention sinks - and shows they are architectural artifacts that serve distinct functional roles, with pre-norm configuration enabling their co-occurrence.
AI.motivation: Previous research observed that massive activations (where few tokens have extreme outlier activations) and attention sinks (where certain tokens attract disproportionate attention) frequently co-occur in Transformer models, often involving the same tokens, but the functional roles and causal relationship between these phenomena remained unclear.
AI.method: The authors conducted systematic experiments to investigate the relationship between these phenomena, identifying the pre-norm configuration as a key architectural choice. They used ablation studies to decouple the phenomena and analyze their distinct functional roles.
AI.result: The study found that massive activations and attention sinks are largely architectural artifacts of modern Transformer design. They serve related but distinct functions: massive activations operate globally by inducing near-constant hidden representations that persist across layers (acting as implicit parameters), while attention sinks operate locally by modulating attention outputs across heads and biasing toward short-range dependencies. Ablating the pre-norm configuration causes the two phenomena to decouple.
AI.conclusion: The co-occurrence of massive activations and attention sinks in Transformer language models is an architectural artifact enabled by the pre-norm configuration, with each phenomenon serving distinct functional roles - massive activations as global implicit parameters and attention sinks as local attention modulators.

title: Axiomatic On-Manifold Shapley via Optimal Generative Flows
summary: Shapley-based attribution is critical for post-hoc XAI but suffers from off-manifold artifacts due to heuristic baselines. While generative methods attempt to address this, they often introduce geometric inefficiency and discretization drift. We propose a formal theory of on-manifold Aumann-Shapley attributions driven by optimal generative flows. We prove a representation theorem establishing the gradient line integral as the unique functional satisfying efficiency and geometric axioms, notably reparameterization invariance. To resolve path ambiguity, we select the kinetic-energy-minimizing Wasserstein-2 geodesic transporting a prior to the data distribution. This yields a canonical attribution family that recovers classical Shapley for additive models and admits provable stability bounds against flow approximation errors. By reframing baseline selection as a variational problem, our method experimentally outperforms baselines, achieving strict manifold adherence via vanishing Flow Consistency Error and superior semantic alignment characterized by Structure-Aware Total Variation. Our code is on https://github.com/cenweizhang/OTFlowSHAP.

AI.tldr: Proposes a formal theory of on-manifold Aumann-Shapley attributions using optimal generative flows to eliminate off-manifold artifacts in XAI, with provable theoretical guarantees and improved empirical performance.
AI.motivation: Existing Shapley-based attribution methods suffer from off-manifold artifacts due to heuristic baseline selection. While generative methods attempt to address this, they introduce geometric inefficiency and discretization drift, creating a need for theoretically sound on-manifold attribution methods.
AI.method: Develops a formal theory of on-manifold Aumann-Shapley attributions driven by optimal generative flows. Proves a representation theorem establishing gradient line integrals as unique solutions satisfying efficiency and geometric axioms. Resolves path ambiguity by selecting the kinetic-energy-minimizing Wasserstein-2 geodesic transporting prior to data distribution.
AI.result: Creates a canonical attribution family that recovers classical Shapley for additive models and provides provable stability bounds against flow approximation errors. Experimentally outperforms baselines with strict manifold adherence (vanishing Flow Consistency Error) and superior semantic alignment (Structure-Aware Total Variation).
AI.conclusion: The proposed optimal generative flow approach provides a theoretically sound solution to the off-manifold attribution problem, reframing baseline selection as a variational problem with superior empirical performance and formal guarantees for on-manifold Shapley attributions.

title: Decoupling Task and Behavior: A Two-Stage Reward Curriculum in Reinforcement Learning for Robotics
summary: Deep Reinforcement Learning is a promising tool for robotic control, yet practical application is often hindered by the difficulty of designing effective reward functions. Real-world tasks typically require optimizing multiple objectives simultaneously, necessitating precise tuning of their weights to learn a policy with the desired characteristics. To address this, we propose a two-stage reward curriculum where we decouple task-specific objectives from behavioral terms. In our method, we first train the agent on a simplified task-only reward function to ensure effective exploration before introducing the full reward that includes auxiliary behavior-related terms such as energy efficiency. Further, we analyze various transition strategies and demonstrate that reusing samples between phases is critical for training stability. We validate our approach on the DeepMind Control Suite, ManiSkill3, and a mobile robot environment, modified to include auxiliary behavioral objectives. Our method proves to be simple yet effective, substantially outperforming baselines trained directly on the full reward while exhibiting higher robustness to specific reward weightings.

AI.tldr: Two-stage reward curriculum that first trains on simplified task rewards, then introduces behavioral objectives, significantly outperforming direct training on full rewards.
AI.motivation: Deep RL for robotics struggles with designing effective reward functions for multi-objective tasks, requiring precise tuning of objective weights to achieve desired behaviors.
AI.method: Decouple task-specific objectives from behavioral terms. Train agent first on simplified task-only rewards for effective exploration, then gradually introduce full reward with auxiliary behavioral terms. Use transition strategies with sample reuse between phases.
AI.result: Method significantly outperforms baselines trained directly on full reward across DeepMind Control Suite, ManiSkill3, and mobile robot environments. Shows higher robustness to specific reward weightings and training stability.
AI.conclusion: Two-stage reward curriculum with decoupled objectives is a simple yet effective approach for learning policies with desired characteristics in multi-objective robotic control tasks.

title: FedBCD:Communication-Efficient Accelerated Block Coordinate Gradient Descent for Federated Learning
summary:
Although Federated Learning has been widely studied in recent years, there are still high overhead expenses in each communication round for large-scale models such as Vision Transformer. To lower the communication complexity, we propose a novel Federated Block Coordinate Gradient Descent (FedBCGD) method for communication efficiency. The proposed method splits model parameters into several blocks, including a shared block and enables uploading a specific parameter block by each client, which can significantly reduce communication overhead. Moreover, we also develop an accelerated FedBCGD algorithm (called FedBCGD+) with client drift control and stochastic variance reduction. To the best of our knowledge, this paper is the first work on parameter block communication for training large-scale deep models. We also provide the convergence analysis for the proposed algorithms. Our theoretical results show that the communication complexities of our algorithms are a factor $1/N$ lower than those of existing methods, where $N$ is the number of parameter blocks, and they enjoy much faster convergence than their counterparts. Empirical results indicate the superiority of the proposed algorithms compared to state-of-the-art algorithms.
  The code is available at https://github.com/junkangLiu0/FedBCGD.

AI.tldr: FedBCGD splits large models into blocks (including shared blocks) and enables clients to upload only specific parameter blocks each round, reducing communication overhead by factor 1/N. An accelerated version (FedBCGD+) adds client drift control and variance reduction.
AI.motivation: Federated Learning suffers from high communication overhead in large-scale models like Vision Transformers during each round, making communication efficiency a critical challenge.
AI.method: Proposes FedBCGD which splits model parameters into blocks (including shared blocks), enabling each client to upload only specific parameter blocks. FedBCGD+ adds accelerated features with client drift control and stochastic variance reduction.
AI.result: Theoretical convergence analysis shows communication complexity reduced by factor 1/N (where N is number of blocks) compared to existing methods. Empirical results demonstrate superiority over state-of-the-art algorithms. First work on parameter block communication for large-scale deep models.
